answer:
**Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Ghazal 33 (opening sher):**

> Dil hi to hai na sang-o-khisht, dard se bhar na aaye kyun?  
> Royenge hum hazaar baar, koi hamein sataaye kyun?[^1]

Translation: "It is a heart, not stone and brick, that fills with pain; / We'll weep a thousand times—why should a soul be slain?"[^2]

Here, *dard* functions as the natural, inevitable **content or fullness** of the human heart—something organic and capacious, like water filling a vessel, contrasting with inert stone. An English-only reader loses the **philosophical inevitability**: "pain" implies pathology or suffering to avoid, not the heart's essential purpose to hold and overflow with sorrow as a mark of life and sensitivity.[^3]

**Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, Ghazal 16 (opening sher):**

> Ek sadma dard-e-dil se meri jaan par toh hai  
> Lekin bala se yaar ke zaanu pe sar toh hai[^4]

Translation: "A blow of heartache strikes me to the core; / But on her knee my head rests evermore, at least."[^5]

Here, *dard-e-dil* acts as a **sudden shock or wound** (*sadma*), a violent impact on the vital core (*jaan*), yet consoled by the beloved's presence. An English-only reader loses the **visceral, martial intensity**: "pain" softens the blow's immediacy and fails to capture the trade-off of agony for intimacy, where the wound is paradoxically life-affirming.[^6]

**Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, Poem 17 (*Dard-e-Ishq*, opening sher):**

> Ae Dard-e-ishq! hai gohar-e-aab-daar tu  
> Na-mehramon mein dekh na ho aashkaar tu[^7]

Translation: "O, Anguish of Love! A pearl of purest water, you; / Be not revealed to the unworthy view."[^8]

Here, *Dard-e-ishq* is personified as a **precious, radiant jewel** (*gohar-e-aab-daar*, a translucent pearl of high value), hidden from the profane. An English-only reader loses the **mystical desirability and luminosity**: "pain" or "anguish" renders it wholly negative, missing its status as a sacred treasure in Sufi love—beautiful, rare, and worth concealing.[^9]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 33 (transliteration), [p-9bcd58](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/33-dil-hi-to-hai-na-sang-o-khisht-dard-se-bhar-na-aay/transliteration/#p-9bcd58).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-d9d50d](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/33-dil-hi-to-hai-na-sang-o-khisht-dard-se-bhar-na-aay/translation/#p-d9d50d).
[^3]: Ibid., full chapter [translation](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/33-dil-hi-to-hai-na-sang-o-khisht-dard-se-bhar-na-aay/translation/).
[^4]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 16 (transliteration), [p-bf9b97](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/16-ek-sadma-dard-e-dil-se-meri-jaan-par-toh-hai/transliteration/#p-bf9b97).
[^5]: Ibid., [p-65a78a](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/16-ek-sadma-dard-e-dil-se-meri-jaan-par-toh-hai/translation/#p-65a78a).
[^6]: Ibid., full chapter [translation](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/16-ek-sadma-dard-e-dil-se-meri-jaan-par-toh-hai/translation/).
[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 (transliteration), [p-787d3f](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/transliteration/#p-787d3f).
[^8]: Ibid., [p-6d19d8](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-6d19d8).
[^9]: Ibid., full chapter [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/).
